children.name: Corcorax
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the White-winged Chough, a large, social passerine endemic to eastern Australia. Despite its name, it is not a true chough but a member of a unique Australian family. It is a striking black bird with large white wing patches that are conspicuous in flight, and it is renowned for its complex cooperative breeding system.

children.name: Corcorax melanorhamphos
children.description: A large, glossy black bird with large white wing patches, a long curved tail, and a slender, down-curved bill, found in woodlands of eastern Australia. It is a highly social species that lives in groups of four to twenty birds that cooperate to build a remarkable mud nest, defend territories, and raise young. Its high-pitched, whistling calls keep the group in constant contact as they forage on the ground.

children.name: Struthidea
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Apostlebird, a medium-sized, grey-brown bird with a short, stout bill and a gregarious nature that gives it its common name. The name derives from the biblical apostles, referring to their habit of travelling in groups of twelve or so. They are endemic to Australia and are close relatives of the White-winged Chough, sharing the mud-nest building habit.

children.name: Struthidea cinerea
children.description: A dull grey-brown bird with a short, black bill and dark brown wings and tail, found in woodlands and farmlands of eastern Australia. Named for its habit of travelling in small flocks of about twelve individuals, it is a highly social and vocal species that forages on the ground for insects and seeds. It builds a bowl-shaped mud nest on horizontal branches, typically in eucalypt trees.
